input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the text input at the bottom of the screen. They mention that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` worked in .NET 9 but doesn't work in .NET 10. Let me provide guidance on this.

In .NET MAUI, the `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated/removed in newer versions. The recommended approach for .NET 10 (and starting from .NET 9 in some cases) is to use the `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` behavior or handle it differently.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. 

The old `App.Current.On<Android>().UseWindowSoftInputModeAdjust(WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize)` approach no longer works because Android deprecated this in API 30+, and .NET MAUI moved away from it. The newer solution is to use `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll`, which automatically adjusts the view when the keyboard appears, though the API for controlling it has changed in recent versions. In .NET 10 with Android 15, edge-to-edge is enforced by default which removes the old `SOFT_INPUT_ADJUST_RESIZE` approach entirely, so I need to work with `WindowInsets` instead. The practical solution is to rely on `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` being enabled by default, but for chat-style layouts with bottom-anchored input fields, I might need to restructure the layout approach to handle keyboard adjustments properly. Setting up the WindowInsets listener in platform-specific code to handle IME adjustments, though the most straightforward approach for .NET 10 is relying on the default `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` behavior with a resizable layout like Grid, and ensuring the soft input mode is configured in the activity or manifest if needed.
